Like the musician, the poet will consequently have to produce variations that vary the tempo, raise or lower the volume, change the key and other such effects. Here is a list of elements that the serious poet should consider, regrouped from the "don'ts for imagists": Let the neophyte know assonance and alliteration, rhyme immediate and delayed, simple and polyphonic, as a musician would expect to know harmony and counterpoint and all the minutiae of his craft. Don't chop your stuff into separate iambs. Dont' make each line stop dead at the end, and then begin every next line with a heave. Let the beginning of the next line catch the rise of the rhythm wave, unless you want a definite longish pause. Naturally, your rhythmic structure should not destroy the shape of your words, or their natural sound, or their meaning. It is improbable that, at the start, you will be able to get a rhythm structure strong enough to affect them very much though you may fall a victim to all sorts of false stopping due to line ends and caesurae. Let the candidate fill his mind with the finest cadences he can discover, preferably in a foreign language … Let him dissect the lyrics of Goethe coldly into their component sound values, syllables long and short, stressed and unstressed, into vowels and consonants. There is, however, in the best of verse a sort of residue of sound which remains in the ear of the hearer and acts more or less as an organ base. A rhyme must have in it some slight element of surprise if it is to give pleasure; it need not be bizarre or curious, but it must be well used if used at all. In short, behave as a musician, a good musician, when dealing with that phase of your art which has exact parallels in music. The same laws govern, and you are bound by no others. An important aspect in producing such effects is the concept of quantity, which Pound also uses to underline the correspondences between the art of poetry and the art of music. Quantity is the idea that syllables have different lenghts and that these differences can be employed to devise a sort of rhythmical patterning. The concept derives from classical study, but for Pound it meant much more and his observations more than verge on the phonological. The concept of quantity is closely linked with that of vers libre and the discussion that went on about the format in Pound's days, when the form was young and considered unpoetical. As to quantity, it is foolish to suppose that we are incapable of distinguishing a long vowel from a short one, or that we are mentally debarred from ascertaining how many consonants intervene between one vowel and the next. Few phonologists will disagree with Pound today as extensive research in their field has demonstrated the capability of speakers to distinguish between such aspects as duration, voicing, friction, nasalisation, as well as the combined effect of all these together with the place of articulation and the position of the tongue. A simple three-word example derived from the book that taught me the inns and outs of English pronunciation ( Pronunciation for Student Teachers , C. Gussenhoven & A. Broeders. 1981):: cord - cod - cot The three words are of decreasing duration, the allophone of the first word having approximately four times the duration of the last word. This concept can in turn be used to produce a rhythm, or to modify it. The most famous and most quoted example is Pound's "The Return", which Eliot himself quotes as an example of verse that is "really quantitative" (Ezra Pound: His Metrics and his Poems, TCTC) See, they return; ah, see the tentative Movements, and the slow feet The trouble in the pace and the uncertain Wavering! We can try to make a distinction between spondee, dactyls, iambs and trochai, but the main effect is clear without them. There are forced pauses in odd places and the natural stresses of words are placed in awkward positions. The long syllables in the spondee "slow feet" is set off against a succession of four short and unstressed syllables. This effect is also used in the third line, where three short and unstressed syllables follow each other, thus preventing any iambic or trochaic rhythm to establish itself. It is only truly quantitative in that its rhythm is meant to be disruptive so that the rhythm is eventually 'drowned out'. In most other cases rhythm and quantity work together, much like the drummer and the flutist. It is hard to believe that a more appropriate poem can ever be found than "The Return". Consider the following lines (cf. Hugh Kenner, The Pound Era , p. 91): Gods of the winged shoe! With them the silver hounds, sniffing the trace of air! There lines are highly rhythmical. Pound's poem is prophetic - it signals the return of poets who know where to place their feet. The "Wing'd-with-Awe" are the old, muddling poets who had lost the scent of progress, deaf to the sound of their words and insensible or unaware of the rhythm and cadence that a good poem should have. What weaklings these young, timorous vers librists are when compared to the souls of blood, the courageous and bold ancient Greek poets. Pound, however, has picked up the scent again and is not about to let go. In his criticism of modern poetry, Pound would frequently complain of the modern lack of discipline, of technical skill, as for instance below, where he talks about modern practitioners of vers libre : The actual language and phrasing is often as bad as that of our elders without even the excuse that the words are shovelled in to fill the metric pattern or to complete the noise of a rhyme-sound. Whether or not the phrases followed by the followers are musical must be left to the reader's decision. At times I can find a marked metre in 'vers libres', as stale and hackneyed as any pseudo-Swinburnian, at times the writers seem to follow no musical structure whatever. ( Literary Essays , p. 3.) Much of Pound's technical wisdom is reflected in his poetry, whence Eliot says that his prose and poetry should not be read without each other, as they are to a large extent complementary. Another example demonstrates Pound's deep phonological insight: (cf. Hugh Kenner, The Pound Era , p. 91): In nature are signatures needing no verbal tradition, oak leaf never plane leaf. (87/573:609) In the last line, all syllables are "stopped off" by consonants which produces a very disruptive effect. The common phonological effect called linking is thus prevented, and the effect is further enhanced by the unvoiced alveolar, fricative, and plosive which break up the voicing in the first three boundaries. It is a good example of a rhythm forcing itself on the reader, since by the time the last word-boundary is reached, most readers will pronounce the two words separately, disjuncted, even though in normal reading the two words would be linked by voicing which runs from the 'l' in plane up to the 'f' in leaf. In combination with short syllables the effect would be equivalent to that of staccato in music. We are reminded once more, that the serious artist should "In short, behave as a musician, a good musician, when dealing with that phase of your art which has exact parallels in music. The same laws govern, and you are bound by no others." And so the artist is to look for inspiration in both music and this aspect of his work in other poetry, not only in his own language, but in that of other languages too. Or, if he has not the faculty, at the very least he can pay attention to those of his colleagues who do and learn the material second hand from them instead. For that, a serious artist could turn to critics like Pound himself, who made intensive study of Provencal, of Chinese, Japanese, of Latin, Greek, Italian and French poetry. Bearing in mind, that: The melopoeia can be appreciated by a foreigner with a sensitive ear, even though he be ignorant of the language in which the poem is written. It is practically impossible to transfer or translate it from one language to another, save perhaps by divine accident, and for half a line at a time. (p.25) This is because different languages have different sound associations, natural rhythms, stresses and pronunciation features. However, the ultimate goal in this aspect of poetry is producing a significant relationship between sound and sense, and as this effect occurs in other languages too, it can be studied with benefit to the serious artist, who will gain a broader and deeper insight into the fundamental properties and possibilities of language in general. These will help him in mastering these possibilities of his own language. Finally, it can be noted that there are basically three kinds of melopoeia: that made to be sung on a tune that made to be intoned or sung to a sort of chant that made to be spoken Although the third is by now far the most common form, it is nevertheless important to be aware of the three different kinds, because "the art of conjoining words in each of these kinds is different, and cannot be clearly understood until the reader knows that there are three different objectives." The main difference between the three different forms is the prominence of the rhythm. In the third, the natural rhythm of spoken language itself will most prominently determine the musical property of the word and hence "the art of conjoining" or phonetic qualities, syllable length and so forth, much like in the modern poem; whereas in the first these features will largely be determined by an external musical pattern which is imposed on the text, and will call for syllables to be drawn out and modulated. This finds its equivalent in the modern pop-song, opera and musical. The second comes inbetween, where the interplay between rhythm and language is less exact, more equal. Usually a drum beat will determine the rhythm. Most of the standard features of the spoken text remain exact, but some may be stressed and reinforced to accompany and stress the rhythm, and alterations in the intonation patterns will occur. A modern equivalent will be found in rap, older forms include religious chants such as found in Africa. One important consequences of this insight has been mentioned before: When you have words of a lament set to the rhythm and tempo of There'll be a Hot Time in the Old Town to-night you have either an intentional burlesque or you have rotten art. (51) As you see, even rhyme patterns have their histories and associations, and these should not be left unconsidered. For more, see logopoeia , below. Phanopoeia - "which is a casting of images upon the visual imagination" If melopoeia concerns itself with the ear, phanopoeia concerns itself with the eye. Note that this does not include the layout and form of the poem, which is part of architectonics , or the form of the whole. Phanopoeia covers anything from simple and straightforward description to complex symbols and use of metaphor, although in poetry it will specifically concern itself with the latter; for the most simple and straightforward manner of describing something is prose. Groundrule: "To use absolutely no word that does not contribute to the presentation" ( Literary Essays , p. 3) The subject of phanopoeia is too wide and too diverse to treat anywhere near exhaustive. Therefore, Pound concentrates on formulating a few of the most important issues involved. These primaly concern the problem of presentation: When Shakespeare talks of the 'Dawn in russet mantle clad' he presents something which the painter does not present. There is in this line of his nothing that one can call description; he presents. ( ibid. , p. 6) In other words, Shakespeare's dawn is not described, she walks onto the stage. This is closely linked to the Aristotalian method of metaphor, in the form which Pound likes best: Aristotle will tell you that 'the apt use of metaphor, being as it is, the swift perception of relations, is the true hall-mark of genius.' That abundance, that readiness of the figure is indeed one of the surest proofs that the mind is up borne on the emotional surge. By apt use, I should say it were well to understand, a swiftness, almost a violence, and certainly a vividness. This does not mean elaboration and complication.( ibid ., 52) Precision is what counts. Great art is a combination of saying something new or saying something in a new way, and saying something "in such a way that one cannot re-say it more effectively." The trick is to use exactly as much words as you need and not one word more. Although it must be remembered that sometimes a certain amount of background noise can help to offset the primary image, in which case however these words are also necessary as precisely that - background material. Regardless of which method of presentation is being used, two distinctions of method can be made. Or, to put it differently, the presentation can have two different attributes. These are, for a lack of better terms, hardness and softness . The qualities are somewhat hard to describe, but Pound gives a few examples and as he shows a clear prediliction for hard verse, we can derive much of the rest: hardness is a certain kind of minimalism in reproducing or representing the poetic subject. In the writers of the duo-cento and early tre-cento we find a precise psychology, embedded in a now almost unintelligible jargon, but there nevertheless. If we cannot get back to these things; if the serious artist cannot attain this precision in verse, then he must either take to prose or give up his claim to being a serious artist. (53) "Use no superfluous word and no adjective which does not reveal something. (7) One moves the reader only by clarity. In depicting the motions of the 'human heart' the durability of the writing depends on the exactitude. (22) litterati - when their very medium, the very essence of their work, the application of word to things goes rotten, i.e. becomes slushy and inexact, or excessive or bloated, the whole machinery of social and of individual thought and order goes to rot. This is a lesson of history, and a lesson not yet half learned. (21) As the most efficient way to say nothing is to keep quiet, and as technique consists precisely in doing the thing that one sets out to do, in the most efficient manner, no man who takes three pages to say nothing can expect to be seriously considered as a technician. To take three pages to say nothing is not style, in the serious sense of that word. (54) Rimbaud brought back to phanopoeia its clarity and directness (35) Learn to charge words from Flaubert (idem) "Consider the definiteness of Dante's presentation as compared with Milton's. (9) In the art of Daniel and Cavalcanti, I have seen that precision which I miss in the Victorians, that explicit rendering, be it of external nature, or of emotion. Their testimony is of the eyewitness, their symptoms are first-hand. (10) It is precisely because of this fustian that the Parnassiads and epics of the eighteenth century and most of the present-day works of most of our contemporary versifiers are pests and abominations. (53) As for the nineteenth century, with all respect to its achievements, I think we shall look back upon it as a rather blurry, messy sort of period, a rather sentimentalistic, mannerish sort of period. (10) And undoubtedly, the list can be made much longer. Perhaps the strongest evidence of Pound's insistence on this matter is that the first of the three groundrules he established for imagism together with "H.D." and Richard Aldington, is virtually identical to the second: Groundrule: "Direct treatment of the 'thing' whether subjective or objective." ( Literary Essays , p. 3) As you saw in the list, again Pound invites us to consider what men in other languages have done before us, for Phanopoeia can ... be translated almost, or wholly, intact. When it is good enough, it is practically impossible for the translator to destroy it save by very crass bungling, and the neglect of perfectly well-known and formulative rules. Logopoeia - 'the dance of the intellect among words', that is to say, it employs words not only for their direct meaning, but it takes count in a special way of habits of usage, of the context we expect to find the word, its usual concomitants, of its known acceptances, and of ironical play. It holds the aesthetic content which is peculiarly the domain of verbal manifestation, and cannot possibly be contained in plastics or music. It is the latest come, and perhaps most tricky and undependable mode." This is the mode which reached a definite climax during modernism. Pound credits Laforgue with the (re)discovery of this tricky aspect of poetry. Its popularity and interest was further boosted by works such as the production of great dictionaries and etymological works, Jung's archetypes, Freud's dream interpretations and Frazer's cultural analysis, most notably The Golden Bough . All this was combined with an increasing awareness of the presence of the past in the present strengthened by the awakening belief that the principles of Darwinian evolution existed in areas besides biology. To give an idea of the complexity of this concept in the field of prosody, we can say that logopoeia covers a vast range of linguistic categories, most notably connotation and collocation, among which also punning, all the way through etymology to cultural history, all of which can be used to understand and control the use of words, as well as make them more effective in doing so. It is not for nothing that Pound quotes Frazer's Golden Bough as a book which displays the "implements by which one can pour." ( ibid. , p. 32) Detailed cultural history of ancient mythologies, cult, ritual and their interrelations with Christianity helped to expose the enormous history that hid behind a great number of contemporary cultural phenomenon, and a skilled poet will be able to resuscitate all this with only a few words, thus achieving a much more intense of expression. The trickyness of this mode, however, does not only lie in its complexity but perhaps even more its undependablity. As historical research uncovers the different contexts in which words have been used, we also become aware of word-use being subject to considerable change. The shifty nature of the word and symbol causes different readers to read or see different things; not everyone possesses equal amounts of background knowledge. The partial solution to this aspect of the problem of logopoeia is closely linked to the work of the dilettante and the serious artist. First, let us remember the general idea that lies behind the work of both, the study of his predecessors. It is a foolish thing for a man to begin his work on a too narrow foundation, it is a disgraceful thing for a man's work not to show steady growth and increased fineness from first to last. (9) As for 'adaptations'; one finds that all the old masters of painting recommend to their pupils that they begin by copying masterwork and proceed to their own composition.(9) Now, consider the following point: "Be influenced by as many great artists as you can, but have the decency either to acknowledge the debt outright or try to conceal it. Besides showing you are a decent as well as a serious artist and work in accordance with (current) practice in most scientific research and publication, there is obviously another advantage to acknowledging your debt: by somehow indicating your source-material, by somehow identifying the works of your predecessors that have helped you create your own work, the reader will be in the possession of a key to unlock the precedent work on which the newer work builds. Through being able to check the sourcetext of the work that the dilettante builds upon, the reader who lacks suficient background knowledge (or possesses too much, for that matter) will be able to figure out what the artist was up to, and also perhaps determine whether he has succeeded or failed. Again, a final word of advice from Pound on the study of other languages: "Logopoeia does not translate; although the attitude of mind it expresses may pass through a paraphrase. Or one might say, you can not translate it ‘locally’, but having determined the original author’s state of mind, you may or may not be able to find a derivative or an equivalent" (25). Architectonics - "the form of the whole." "Any work of art is a compound of freedom and order. It is perfectly obvious that art hangs between chaos on the one side and mechanics on the other. A pedantic insistence upon detail tends to drive out 'major form'. A firm hold on major form makes for a freedom of detail. In painting men intent on minutiae gradually lost the sense of form and form-combination. The form of the whole is not just the shape of the poem - the sonnet, or the lyric, or the ballad, the epic poem, the Sapphic ode, the alexandrine, the heroic couplet, and so on. Rather, it is a kind of unity that works on all levels mentioned so far. Consider a castle with magnificent towers, which, however, are far too large in comparison with the puny walls to which they form the corner. Consider a musical piece that consists of a number of near-perfect technical and compositional gems, without, however, any larger division in movements or any dramatic development. Consider a play full of brilliant jokes but no plot. Or, consider a painting that whether you look at it from afar or with a magnifying glass, does not disclose anything other than beautiful little round dots with a chaotic pattern. None of these examples are bad art in themselves; their success depends on whether the artist achieves the effect he set out to create. What is important, is the consideration of form in creating the work of art. If, for instance, a serious artist wished to create a sonnet, then he should have an idea to express which can be fitted precisely into a sonnet. It is no use fitting the idea in the couplet and then fill up the octet and sextet with meaningless detail that does nothing to enhance the effect of the couplet. Or, to turn the matter around, when an idea taps you on the shoulder, turn around and look at it carefully to see what kind of form it would wear most gracefully, that would not only fit the idea tightly, but could even contribute to the expression, the intensity of that idea. Conclusion What I've been able to do here is little more than make a coherent sketch of Pound's theoretical heritage. Perhaps such coherence was not always apparent to readers, but I believe that those who were familiar enough with his work, like Eliot, were able to see enough of a unity to be profoundly influenced. There were undoubtedly other poets, and perhaps this overview will help to identify them and determine how far the influence went; where the disagreements were and so forth. One poet who was at least partially under the influence of Pound was Marianne Moore, evidence of which is given in The Pound Era . Eliot, however, was perhaps closest to Pound and perhaps also most strongly influenced. There are numerous effects in Eliot's poetry, such as the working of shards of popular songs, extensive allusions to and borrowings from influences, use of various rhythmical formats and metrical effects, foreign influences, and much more. We see Eliot's attack on vers libre, so much like Pound's attack on vers libre, with the same arguments. We read essays like The Music of Poetry, which, 30 years after Pound started formulating and stressing the important and instructive similarities between the two media, are little more than a rehashing of what Pound said, in a milder form. An essay like Tradition and the Individual Talent is, in the light of this reformulation, little more than a somewhat romantic rephrasing of Pound's idea that an artist should know the work of his predecessors, on a slightly larger scale. Almost conform to Pound's criterium of the dilettante , Eliot acknowledges his debt in the last paragraph of part I of the essay: There remains to define this process of depersonalization and its relation to the sense of tradition. It is in this depersonalization that art may be said to approach the condition of science. I shall, therefore, invite you to consider, as a suggestive analogy, the action which takes place when a bit of finely filiated platinum is introduced into a chamber containing oxygen and sulphur dioxide. Like Pound, Eliot makes an analogy between the work of art and of science. He stresses the importance of the past in relation to the present. His focus on 'what is already living' is congruous with Pound's idea that poetry should be written in the medium of the present, unless there is a very good reason to revive a medium from the past (for instance the stricture of the quatrain form as a counterforce to excesses of the diluters of bad vers libre ). Certainly, there are influences from Bradley's concept of history, and perhaps even from Joyce, who occasionally mentions the concept of personalisation for instance in Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man , not to mention Wordsworth's introduction to the Lyrical Ballads . But the process of thinking about the art of poetry in this manner is unmistakably in the style of Pound's serious artist. Perhaps the strongest evidence of Pound's influence in this respect is Eliot's statement that his critical writing was more or less a side-effect of his poetry-writing. It shows that Eliot was thinking about his poetry in a deliberate, conscious, and near scientific manner much in agreement with Pound's definition of the Serious Artist. There is much more, but that lies beyond the scope of this essay, which intends to provide a point of reference and a springboard for further research. An attempt to pave the way for other serious artists - or critics for that matter.
